perturbation.original_text: Except as otherwise specifically provided herein, ExxonMobil agrees to pay FCE the amount of each invoice under this Agreement within thirty (30) days following ExxonMobil's receipt. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if ExxonMobil has a good faith dispute regarding any amounts invoiced by FCE, ExxonMobil may withhold payment for the disputed amount, provided that ExxonMobil pays the undisputed amount and notifies FCE in writing of the specific amount and nature of the dispute promptly upon receipt of FCE's invoice in which case the Parties shall attempt to resolve the dispute in good faith. The Parties shall endeavor to resolve such dispute within fifteen (15) days of notice of the dispute, and ExxonMobil shall remit payment to FCE within fifteen (15) days of resolution of such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as otherwise specifically provided herein, ExxonMobil agrees to pay FCE the amount of each invoice under this Agreement within sixty (60) days following ExxonMobil's receipt. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if ExxonMobil has a good faith dispute regarding any amounts invoiced by FCE, ExxonMobil may withhold payment for the disputed amount, provided that ExxonMobil pays the undisputed amount and notifies FCE in writing of the specific amount and nature of the dispute promptly upon receipt of FCE's invoice in which case the Parties shall attempt to resolve the dispute in good faith. The Parties shall endeavor to resolve such dispute within thirty (45) days of notice of the dispute, and ExxonMobil shall remit payment to FCE within thirty (45) days of resolution of such dispute.
perturbation.explanation: Extended payment terms to 60 days and dispute resolution to 45 days could violate prompt payment laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York State Prompt Payment Law for Construction Contracts
perturbation.law_explanation: NY law states that payments be made no later than 30 days after approval of the invoice, a term that is longer than the 60 days in the contract, potentially violating the law. The Perturbation extends the payment window. The law requires disputes to be resolved within 15 days of notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: New York General Business Law § 756-A (2022) - Obligations. :: 2022 New York Laws :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of the New York Laws 2024 N.Y. Laws 2023 N.Y. Laws 2022 N.Y. Laws (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 New York Laws GBS - General Business Article 35-E - Construction Contracts 756-A - Obligations. Universal Citation: NY Gen Bus L § 756-A (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 756-a. Obligations. It is the policy and purpose of this article to expedite payment of all monies owed to those who perform contracting services pursuant to construction contracts. Except as otherwise provided in this article, the terms and conditions of a construction contract shall supersede the provisions of this article and govern the conduct of the parties thereto. 1. Billing cycle. The parties to a construction contract may, by mutual agreement, establish a billing cycle for the submission of invoices requesting payment for work performed pursuant to a construction contract. In the absence of an agreement by the parties as to the billing cycle, the billing cycle shall be the calendar month within which the work is performed. 2. Invoices. (a) A contractor shall be entitled to invoice the owner for interim payments at the end of the billing cycle. A contractor shall be entitled to submit a final invoice for payment in full upon the performance of all the contractor's obligation under the contract. (i) Upon delivery of an invoice and all contractually required documentation, an owner shall approve or disapprove all or a portion of such invoice within twelve business days. Owner approval of invoices shall not be unreasonably withheld nor shall an owner, in bad faith disapprove all or a portion of an invoice. If an owner declines to approve an invoice or a portion thereof, it must prepare and issue a writ
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the payment terms to 60 days and dispute resolution to 45 days. The Law Explanation states NY law requires payments be made no later than 30 days after invoice approval and disputes resolved within 15 days. This extension directly contradicts the suggested prompt payment law.

perturbation.original_text: FCE will keep, or cause to be kept, true books, records, and accounts in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles and containing all information necessary for the accurate determination of all amounts payable to FCE under this Agreement, and any other obligations under this Agreement. Such books, records and accounts will be maintained for a period of at least three (3) years following the termination or expiration of this Agreement, provided there are no pending disputes between the Parties. In the case of a dispute, the books, records, and accounts will be maintained for one (1) year following resolution of such dispute.
perturbation.changed_text: FCE will keep, or cause to be kept, true books, records, and accounts in accordance with Generally Accepted Accounting Principles and containing all information necessary for the accurate determination of all amounts payable to FCE under this Agreement, and any other obligations under this Agreement. Such books, records and accounts will be maintained for a period of at least one (1) year following the termination or expiration of this Agreement, provided there are no pending disputes between the Parties. In the case of a dispute, the books, records, and accounts will be maintained for six (6) months following resolution of such dispute.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the record retention period to one year and six months during a dispute could violate federal tax regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 26 U.S. Code § 6001 - Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns
perturbation.law_explanation: IRS regulations require records to be kept as long as they may be relevant to tax administration. Reducing the retention period to one year, particularly during disputes, could hinder IRS audits and violate federal law requiring adequate record keeping for tax purposes. The modification does not comply with IRS guidelines regarding record retention.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 18 - RECORDS AND AUDIT, Paragraph 18.01 Recordkeeping

Notice or regulations requiring records, statements, and special returns Every person liable for any tax imposed by this title, or for the collection thereof, shall keep such records, render such statements, make such
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the record retention period, potentially contradicting IRS regulations. Snippet 1 (26 USC 6001) states that every person liable for tax shall keep such records. The modification reduces the record retention period, which could hinder IRS audits and violate federal law requiring adequate record keeping for tax purposes. The original text states three years after termination, or one year after dispute resolution, while the modified text stipulates one year after termination, or six months after dispute resolution. This reduction may not comply with IRS guidelines, making the terms inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: Unless the defaulting Party has remedied such default within the Cure Period, this Agreement may be terminated, without penalty, payment or prejudice to claims then accrued, by written notice to the defaulting Party by the non-defaulting Party specifying the date of termination which will be of immediate effect. In the event of termination under this Paragraph 12.03 where FCE is the defaulting Party, FCE's royalty-free licenses described in Paragraph 7.01(b)(1), 8.02(b)(1)(i), 8.02 (b)(1)(ii), 8.02(b)(1)(iii), and 8.02(b)(2)(i) will immediately convert to royalty-bearing licenses, with the royalty rate to be negotiated by the Parties in good faith.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless the defaulting Party has remedied such default within the Cure Period, this Agreement may be terminated, without penalty, payment or prejudice to claims then accrued, by written notice to the defaulting Party by the non-defaulting Party specifying the date of termination which will be of immediate effect. In the event of termination under this Paragraph 12.03 where FCE is the defaulting Party, all licenses granted to FCE will immediately terminate.
perturbation.explanation: The elimination of a right to transition to royalty-bearing licenses upon FCE default may violate bankruptcy law.
perturbation.law_explanation: In the event of bankruptcy, 11 U.S.C. § 365(n) protects the rights of licensees of intellectual property. By eliminating the possibility of a transition to royalty-bearing licenses, the modified clause could be deemed an attempt to circumvent these protections, potentially violating federal bankruptcy law.
perturbation.location: ARTICLE 12 - TERM AND TERMINATION, Paragraph 12.03 Failure to Perform

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 11 U.S. Code § 365 - Executory contracts and unexpired leases | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 11 U.S. Code § 365 - Executory contracts and unexpired leases U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Except as provided in sections 765 and 766 of this title and in subsections (b), (c), and (d) of this section, the trustee, subject to the court’s approval, may assume or reject any executory contract or unexpired lease of the debtor. (b) (1) If there has been a default in an executory contract or unexpired lease of the debtor, the trustee may not assume such contract or lease unless, at the time of assumption of such contract or lease, the trustee— (A) cures, or provides adequate assurance that the trustee will promptly cure, such default other than a default that is a breach of a provision relating to the satisfaction of any provision (other than a penalty rate or penalty provision) relating to a default arising from any failure to perform nonmonetary obligations under an unexpired lease of real property, if it is impossible for the trustee to cure such default by performing nonmonetary acts at and after the time of assumption, except that if such default arises from a failure to operate in accordance with a nonresidential real property lease, then such default shall be cured by performance at and after the time of assumption in accordance with such lease, and pecuniary losses resulting from such default shall be compensated in accordance with the provisions of this paragraph; (B) compensates, or provides adequate assurance that the trustee will promptly compensate, a party other than the debtor to such contract or lease, for any actual pecuniary loss to such party resulting from such default; and (C) provides adequate assurance of future performance under such contract or lease. (2) Paragraph (1) of this subsection does not apply to a default that is a breach of a provision relatin
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a legal contradiction by eliminating FCE's right to transition to royalty-bearing licenses upon default, which potentially violates federal bankruptcy law, specifically 11 U.S.C. § 365(n) (as suggested in the Law Explanation). While the snippets provided (11 U.S.C. § 365) do not explicitly detail the protection of licensees' rights in intellectual property during bankruptcy, they establish the trustee's power to assume or reject executory contracts. The original text provided for a transition to royalty-bearing licenses, which could be argued to be a protected right in bankruptcy. The changed text eliminates this option, which could be viewed as an attempt to circumvent bankruptcy law protections, thus creating a legal contradiction.
